channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: First Sonic The Hedgehog 4 Teaser Confirms Jim Carrey Is Back
item.content:encoded:
We still don’t know much about Sonic the Hedgehog 4, but one of the big questions fans have had about the film is whether or not Jim Carrey would return as Doctor Eggman given his fate at the end of Sonic the Hedgehog 3. Now, we have our first teaser for the film, and it not only confirms Carrey’s return, but that Keanu Reeves’ Shadow will be back as well.

We don’t get any real footage of the film in the teaser, but we do get a voiceover from Carrey saying, “You think your world is safe, but your time is about to run out. The chaos has only just begun.” As the egghead gives his speech, we see the Chaos Emeralds, each with flashes of different characters inside, including Amy, Tails, Knuckles, and Sonic’s human parents Maddie and Tom. Shadow also makes an appearance, and Reeves has been confirmed to return in the film.

The big reveal here is that Carrey is returning as Eggman, who appeared to have died at the end of Sonic the Hedgehog 3, sacrificing himself to stop his grandfather’s plan to destroy the world. There have been rumors that Sonic 4 will take place at least partially in a different timeline in which Eggman has conquered the world. Amy is supposedly from this alternate universe, which would explain how Carrey’s mad scientist could be both alive and also still an antagonist given the original’s change of heart at the end of the third movie.

Also joining the cast are Ben Kingsley and Nick Offerman, but we don’t know what roles they’ll be playing just yet.

Sonic the Hedgehog 4 is coming to theaters a year from now on March 19, 2027, and will star Frozen and Good Place actor Kristen Bell as Amy.



https://kotaku.com/sonic-hedgehog-4-teaser-amy-robotnik-jim-carrey-kristen-bell-2000680296
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Crimson Desert Studio Investors See ‘Generally Favorable’ Reviews And Race To Dump The Stock As It Plummets 30 Percent
item.content:encoded:
The stock price of Pearl Abyss Corp has fallen by, as of this writing, roughly 29.88 percent today, from 65,600 KRW (South Korean won) down to 46,000 KRW. The cause? I’m not a finance journalist, but it seems pretty clear that the reviews of Pearl Abyss’s massively hyped action RPG, Crimson Desert, might have something to do with it, as it’s currently sitting at an 80 on OpenCritic and 78 on Metacritic.

Still, those are pretty decent scores. Obviously not quite on the level of the high 90s that some expectant fans were hoping for, but is a 4 out of 5 average really enough to tank Pearl Abyss’s stock valuation that hard, or is there more to this story? Probably not, honestly. Trust me, I know it sounds a little ridiculous that Crimson Desert only being pretty good, as opposed to really good, could wipe out 30 percent of the stock price, but it does make sense when you take a look at the whole picture.

From December 30, 2025, to January 30, 2026, Pearl Abyss Corp’s stock price shot up by roughly 52.94 percent, from 37,400 KRW to 57,200 KRW. So what happened in that window that caused it to suddenly jump? Pearl Abyss announced that Crimson Desert had “gone gold” on January 22, which meant that the game would definitely meet its March 19 release date.

As Pearl Abyss is a publicly traded company, this caused investors to swarm the stock and increase its valuation. From here, Crimson Desert’s cleverly executed PR push resulted in the stock climbing higher and higher, until Pearl Abyss Corp’s share price hit an all-time high of 68,500 KRW on March 16; roughly a 125-percent increase when compared to its valuation on March 16, 2025.

Long story short, a bunch of investors bought in just before Crimson Desert’s hype reached fever pitch, then sold off their stocks this morning as soon as markets closed. But does this spell doom for Pearl Abyss? Nah, probably not. The company’s stock price is still currently higher than it was the year before, and things could bounce back pretty quickly once we know Crimson Desert’s sales numbers. We’ll be able to confirm exactly how much those review scores affected sales when the game is available on Steam in roughly eight hours.



https://kotaku.com/crimson-desert-reviews-pearl-abyss-stock-prices-metacritic-2000680294
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Report: Sony Is Planning To Ditch ‘PlayStation Network’ As A Brand
item.content:encoded:
Insider Gaming is reporting that it’s seen emailed plans by Sony to abandon the “PlayStation Network” and “PSN” brands by the end of the summer. According to the report, by September 2026 PSN will become a thing of the past, in what’s described as a “purely visual” change.

It seems this is primarily an exercise in tidying up, trying to remove one of PlayStation’s many brands that perhaps don’t really make a lot of sense to customers. The PlayStation Network is the term used for Sony’s online gaming offerings (including the PlayStation Store), first used in 2006 for the PS3, before spreading across a range of Sony hardware, and was used to distinguish between the activities that took place using the internet and those that occured when just sticking a Blu-ray disc into a drive. Today, it’s honestly a bit of a confusing delineation in a gaming world where pretty much everything is “online” in some form, and the distinction is barely perceivable.

Sony’s internal email reflects the same thoughts, explaining that the de-branding will not cause any technical changes. “To simplify and unify branding, the terms ‘PlayStation Network’ and ‘PSN’ will be phased out across all SIE assets by September 2026,” Insider quotes. “All features currently associated with PSN, including core network features such as friends, multiplayer, and trophies, will remain unaffected and available to players.”

PlayStation Lifestyle has noticed this process may have already begun, spotting that in the Network options on the PS5 the term “PlayStation Network” has been replaced with just “PlayStation” and the PSN logo nixed from the menu, and that the PlayStation Network Status page is now simply “PlayStation Status.”

It all seems smart enough. It’s reasonable to assume most PlayStation users associate the PlayStation Plus brand with being able to play games online and in multiplayer, while no one at all would ever consider access to a digital store as something that even needs a label. “PSN” has been a part of our lexicon for so long that it feels strange that it won’t be around any more, but the chances are we’ll only notice it’s gone when someone reminds us in around November.

Of course, it would be far better if we existed on the timeline where none of Sony, Microsoft or Nintendo had considered online gaming to be something they could monetize with paywalls and branding. But despite the PC providing no such barriers, it seems that part’s not going anyway any time soon.



https://kotaku.com/report-sony-is-planning-to-ditch-playstation-network-as-a-brand-2000680276
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
